text.title: Gcp Org Policies Core Rules
text.summary: 5 testable rules for the gcp-org-policies methodology. Each rule pairs a binary check + sourced rationale + concrete remediation hook.

rule.statement: Production organisations MUST set `iam.disableServiceAccountKeyCreation` at org level; explicit allow-list exceptions only.
rule.rationale: Service-account JSON keys are the highest-impact credential-leak vector; the org-level constraint blocks the default footgun.

rule.statement: Workloads under data-residency requirements MUST enforce `gcp.resourceLocations` at the relevant folder.
rule.rationale: Without the constraint, engineers can accidentally provision resources outside the allowed regions and violate compliance.

rule.statement: Production folders SHOULD restrict the list of allowed services via `serviceuser.services` to the inventory the team operates.
rule.rationale: Restricting allowed services prevents shadow-IT API enablement and contains the supported surface.

rule.statement: Policy inheritance + exceptions MUST be documented per folder/project (`docs/org-policies.md`).
rule.rationale: Inherited policies are easy to forget; documentation prevents debugging mysteries during incident response.

rule.statement: New constraints MUST be rolled out in `dry-run` mode for ≥1 week before enforcement.
rule.rationale: Dry-run surfaces violating workloads without breaking them; direct enforcement risks production outage.

rule.statement: If the input fails ALL "Applies If" preconditions or matches ANY "Skip If" disqualifier, do not apply this methodology — route the task to the adjacent sibling instead.
rule.rationale: Even flat checklists need an explicit skip leaf in the decision tree; this rule is the universal terminal.
